channel.title: Transactions on Chen Kai Blog
channel.description: Recent content in Transactions on Chen Kai Blog

item.title: Databases (7): Distributed Transactions — 2PC, Saga, and Why Consensus Is Hard
item.description:
Everything we covered about transactions in Article 3 assumed a single database server. One machine, one transaction log, one lock manager. The moment your data lives on more than one machine — which happens the moment you shard, use microservices with separate databases, or replicate with strong consistency — you face the hardest problem in distributed systems: how do you make multiple machines agree?

The Distributed Transaction Problem

Consider an e-commerce system with separate services for orders and inventory, each with its own database:

item.title: Databases (3): Transactions and Concurrency — ACID, Isolation Levels, and Locking
item.description:
Every application that handles money, inventory, or any state that matters eventually hits a concurrency bug. Two users buy the last item in stock. A bank transfer debits one account but crashes before crediting the other. A report reads half-updated data and produces nonsense numbers. Transactions exist to prevent these failures, and understanding how they work is non-negotiable for anyone building production systems.

What Is a Transaction?

A transaction is a group of operations that the database treats as a single unit. Either all operations succeed, or none of them do.
